summary: Dan Shapiro details the final construction steps of a custom coffee table with inlaid slate tiles. The post covers leg construction from laminated 6/4 stock, attachment techniques using hidden screws beneath the slate, and slate tile preparation from inexpensive Home Depot tiles. The bulk of the article focuses on finishing with shellac, including dissolving flakes, pore-filling with pumice, and a simplified French polish technique. The post is written as a practical woodworking tutorial with humorous asides about bug excretions, potable alcohol, and using a red sock as a finishing pad.
key_points: Leg construction uses laminated 6/4 stock glued in two directions, with a 4/4 cap on the bottom to minimize exposed endgrain
Screws attaching legs to the tabletop are cleverly hidden beneath the slate tile inlays
Shellac finishing involves dissolving flakes in denatured alcohol, filling pores with pumice, then building up layers using a simplified French polish method
Inexpensive ungauged slate tiles from Home Depot are mounted with clear caulk piles that allow future replacement
money_quotes: Once this dries up, nothing short of a flaming jackhammer will cause your table distress.
Technically you can return the grinder to directly to coffee use, although the spouse might not appreciate you contaminating his/her coffee with bug excretions.
Ironically, the only toxic thing about shellac is the methyl alcohol deliberately added to de-tipple the otherwise potable ethyl alcohol.
If you have a source of 100% pure ethanol, you can use that instead of denatured alcohol for a great finish (and a hell of a party).
In a moment of bizarre experimentation, I decided to apply the shellac with a red wool sock wrapped in an old T-shirt. It worked, but my table smells like an old foot.
Jointing endgrain is one of the sketchiest jointing tasks.
key_insight: Building quality furniture is accessible with basic materials and techniques, and the imperfect, personality-filled process is as much the point as the finished product.
